messages.content: please edit this text :"This research utilizes various verification methods, such as PIT and rank histograms, reliability and ROC diagrams, RSS, BS, BSS, and CRPS, to assess the effectiveness of the ensemble post-processing techniques outlined in subsection 3.2. The testing period ranges from November 28, 2015, to February 28, 2016, during which BMA and EMOS methods are utilized to estimate 24-hour accumulated precipitation predictive PDFs via two approaches: global and semi-local. Observational data is retrieved from 349 irregularly spaced synoptic meteorological stations across Iran Figure 1, with a specified region over Iran shown in Figure 2 for the semi-local method."

This research employs various verification methods, including PIT and rank histograms, reliability and ROC diagrams, RSS, BS, BSS, and CRPS, to evaluate the efficacy of the ensemble post-processing techniques outlined in subsection 3.2. The testing period spans from November 28, 2015, to February 28, 2016. During this period, BMA and EMOS methods are employed to estimate 24-hour accumulated precipitation predictive PDFs using two approaches: global and semi-local. Observational data is obtained from 349 synoptic meteorological stations across Iran (see Figure 1), with a specific region highlighted in Figure 2 for the semi-local method.

Edit this text: "The verification rank histograms for raw ensemble precipitation forecasts are displayed in Figure 3 for lead times of 24 hours, 48 hours, and 72 hours. The histograms indicate a non-uniform distribution with an under-dispersed ensemble system. This suggests that the ensemble members are too similar and not spread out enough (Wilks, 2011)."

Figure 3 presents the verification rank histograms for raw ensemble precipitation forecasts at lead times of 24 hours, 48 hours, and 72 hours. The histograms exhibit a non-uniform distribution, indicating an under-dispersed ensemble system. This suggests that the ensemble members are inadequately diverse and lack sufficient spread (Wilks, 2011).

edit the text:"The PIT diagram is displayed in Figure 4 for both the BMA and EMOS-CSG methods. The PIT histograms assess two versions of global and semi-local predictive PDFs. The histogram displays the predictive CDF evaluated at verifying observations. Compared with the rank histogram of the raw ensemble forecast (Figure 3), it is evident that all post-processing methods implemented have effectively eliminated the under-dispersion displayed by the raw ensemble forecast.  Also, there is not too much difference between global and semi-local methods. It just seems that the semi-local method is slightly more uniform than the global method.  For a more detailed review, the Kolmogorov–Smirnov test results are presented in Table 1. The PIT uniformity test is rejected only for the EMOS-CSG model (24-hour forecasts) in the global approach."

Figure 4 displays the PIT diagram for both the BMA and EMOS-CSG methods. The PIT histograms evaluate two versions of global and semi-local predictive PDFs. The histogram represents the predictive CDF assessed at verifying observations. In comparison to the rank histogram of the raw ensemble forecast (Figure 3), it is clear that all implemented post-processing methods have successfully addressed the under-dispersion exhibited by the raw ensemble forecast. Additionally, there is minimal disparity between the global and semi-local methods, although the semi-local method appears to be slightly more uniform than the global method. For a more comprehensive analysis, the Kolmogorov-Smirnov test results are presented in Table 1. It is worth noting that the PIT uniformity test is only rejected for the EMOS-CSG model (24-hour forecasts) in the global approach.

Edit the text:"The reliability diagrams for global and semi-local probabilistic forecasts of 24-hour accumulated precipitation over lead times of 24, 48, and 72 hours are shown in Figures 5, 6, and 7. The democratic voting (raw ensemble forecast), BMA, EMOS-CSG, and EMOS-GEV methods are utilized for estimating the forecasts. The assessment includes various thresholds of 0.1, 2.5, 5, 10, 15, and 25 mm."

Figures 5, 6, and 7 present the reliability diagrams for global and semi-local probabilistic forecasts of 24-hour accumulated precipitation across lead times of 24, 48, and 72 hours. The forecasts are estimated using the democratic voting (raw ensemble forecast), BMA, EMOS-CSG, and EMOS-GEV methods. The assessment encompasses different thresholds of 0.1, 2.5, 5, 10, 15, and 25 mm.

edit the text:"The reliability diagrams in Figures 5, 6, and 7 indicate that the predictive PDFs estimated by the raw ensemble method are not accurately calibrated, as they tend to align with the “no resolution” line, particularly for higher thresholds. "

The reliability diagrams depicted in Figures 5, 6, and 7 demonstrate that the predictive PDFs estimated by the raw ensemble method are not well-calibrated. Specifically, they tend to align closely with the "no resolution" line, particularly for higher thresholds.

edit the text: "For the 24-hour, 48-hour, and 72-hour forecasts and all thresholds, the semi-local method outperforms the global method for the three techniques used. Only for BMA method and threshold of 0.1 mm, both the global and semi-local approaches yield comparable results. In semi-local approach, the reliability for light and moderate precipitation levels (0.1, 2.5, 5 and 10 mm) is noteworthy. Moreover, at 15 and 25 mm threshold values, the EMOS-CSG method demonstrates better calibration than the BMA and EMOS-GEV methods. In summary, the semi-local EMOS-CSG method has the most ability for unbiased probability estimation of dichotomous events."

Across all thresholds and forecast timeframes (24-hour, 48-hour, and 72-hour), the semi-local method consistently outperforms the global method for all three techniques employed. There is a notable exception for the BMA method and a threshold of 0.1 mm, where both global and semi-local approaches yield comparable results. Notably, the semi-local approach exhibits remarkable reliability for light and moderate precipitation levels (0.1, 2.5, 5, and 10 mm). Additionally, at threshold values of 15 and 25 mm, the EMOS-CSG method demonstrates superior calibration compared to both the BMA and EMOS-GEV methods. In sum, the semi-local EMOS-CSG method proves to be the most proficient in unbiased probability estimation for dichotomous events.

edit the text:"Figure 8 displays the ROC diagrams illustrating the calibrated forecasts for 24, 48, and 72-hour lead times using the BMA, EMOS-CSG methods, and the raw ensemble forecast across various thresholds. Javanshiri et al. (2020) conducted a comparison between the BMA and EMOS methods. By analyzing ROC diagrams, the authors found compelling evidence that both the BMA and EMOS methods exhibit superior discrimination compared to the raw ensemble forecasts across various lead times and thresholds. Consequently, the order of the methods in terms of discrimination, from highest to lowest, is EMOS-CSG, BMA, and EMOS-GEV."

Figure 8 showcases the ROC diagrams, which depict the calibrated forecasts for lead times of 24, 48, and 72 hours using the BMA, EMOS-CSG methods, and the raw ensemble forecast at different thresholds. In a study by Javanshiri et al. (2020), a comparison was made between the BMA and EMOS methods. The authors discovered compelling evidence in the ROC diagrams, indicating that both the BMA and EMOS methods exhibit superior discrimination compared to the raw ensemble forecasts across various lead times and thresholds. As a result, the methods can be ranked in terms of discrimination, from highest to lowest, as EMOS-CSG, BMA, and EMOS-GEV.

edit the text:"In this paper, the calculation of ROC curves was performed for two methods, namely BMA and EMOS-CSG, using two global and semi-local techniques. As depicted in the diagrams presented in Figure 8, generally, the graphs for both global and semi-local approaches exhibit almost similar pattern except that the global method compared to semi-local method for heavy precipitation level has a better discrimination.  
Furthermore, the calculation of the RSS depicted in Figure 9 demonstrates almost the same results between the global and semi-local methods. However, it is worth noting that in thresholds of 15 and 25 mm, the global method exhibited slightly better performance compared to the semi-local method. However, for the lower thresholds, the RSS values are comparatively lower. This can be attributed to the fact that the raw ensemble forecasts exhibit good discrimination specifically in these thresholds, as illustrated in Figure 9."

In this paper, ROC curves were generated for two methods, namely BMA and EMOS-CSG, using both global and semi-local techniques. The diagrams presented in Figure 8 show that, in general, the graphs for both global and semi-local approaches display a similar pattern. However, the global method tends to exhibit better discrimination than the semi-local method for heavy precipitation levels.  

Additionally, the calculation of the RSS, as demonstrated in Figure 9, yields almost identical results for both the global and semi-local methods. It is noteworthy that, for thresholds of 15 and 25 mm, the global method demonstrates slightly better performance compared to the semi-local method. However, for lower thresholds, the RSS values are comparatively lower. This can be attributed to the fact that the raw ensemble forecasts demonstrate good discrimination specifically at these thresholds, as illustrated in Figure 9.

edit the text:"The figures in Figure 10 present the BS values for the probabilistic forecasts of 24-hour accumulative precipitation at various thresholds, considering lead times of 24, 48, and 72 hours.  In the study conducted by Javanshiri et al. (2020), BMA, EMOS-CSG, and EMOS-GEV approaches were compared using the metrics of BS and BSS. The results revealed that the BS values of BMA and EMOS-CSG were significantly lower compared to EMOS-GEV and the raw forecast for all lead times. However, there was no significant difference between the BS values of BMA and EMOS-CSG methods. Moreover, the BS values remained relatively consistent across all forecast lead times. Furthermore, the BSS values for the 24, 48, and 72-hour forecasts were positive for BMA, EMOS-CSG, and EMOS-GEV methods, indicating that these methods were more calibrated than the raw ensemble forecasts. Notably, the study highlighted that the BMA and EMOS-CSG methods demonstrated greater skill, particularly in the 24-hour lead time forecasts, compared to the EMOS-GEV method. Hence, this article focuses on calculating the BS for the BMA and EMOS-CSG methods using both global and semi-local approaches. The findings indicate that generally, (i) the BS values of the semi-local are less than the global for both the BMA and EMOS_CGS methods, (ii) the BS values of BMA method is less than EMOS-CGS method, (iii) These differences are larger for lower thresholds.
"

The BS values for 24-hour accumulated precipitation probabilistic forecasts at various thresholds and lead times of 24, 48, and 72 hours are presented in Figure 10. In a study conducted by Javanshiri et al. (2020), the BMA, EMOS-CSG, and EMOS-GEV approaches were compared using the metrics of BS and BSS. The results indicated that both the BMA and EMOS-CSG methods had significantly lower BS values compared to EMOS-GEV and the raw forecast for all lead times. However, there was no significant difference observed between the BS values of the BMA and EMOS-CSG methods. The BS values remained relatively consistent across all forecast lead times. 

Furthermore, the BSS values for the 24, 48, and 72-hour forecasts were positive for the BMA, EMOS-CSG, and EMOS-GEV methods, indicating superior calibration compared to the raw ensemble forecasts. Notably, the study highlighted that the BMA and EMOS-CSG methods exhibited greater skill, particularly in the 24-hour lead time forecasts, compared to the EMOS-GEV method. Consequently, this article focuses on calculating the BS for the BMA and EMOS-CSG methods using both global and semi-local approaches. The findings indicate that, in general, (i) the BS values of the semi-local approach are lower than those of the global approach for both the BMA and EMOS-CSG methods, (ii) the BS values of the BMA method are lower than those of the EMOS-CSG method, and (iii) these differences are more pronounced for lower thresholds.

edit the text:"For a more detailed comparison of models performance with raw forecast, the BSS is also calculated (Figure 5). As can be seen, the semi-local method is more skilful than the global method. As well as the BMA model outperforms (underperforms) EMOS-CSG for lower (upper) thresholds."

For a more comprehensive comparison of model performance with the raw forecast, the BSS is also calculated (Figure 5). It is evident that the semi-local method exhibits greater skill than the global method. Additionally, the BMA model outperforms the EMOS-CSG model for lower thresholds, while it underperforms for upper thresholds.

edit the text: "Two semi-local and global approaches to parameter estimation of BMA and EMOS-CSG models for the calibration of precipitation forecasts of WRF system in Iran are applied. The forecast skill of the global method and semi-local method based on the clustering of observation stations for BMA and EMOS-CSG models is evaluated. The consistency, accuracy, skill, reliability, resolution, and discrimination of the four global BMA, semi-local BMA, global EMOS-CSG, and semi-local EMOS-CSG models are measured. To measure these criteria, the PIT, BS, BSS, Reliability diagram, ROC diagram and RSS are used. The results indicate that overall, the clustering-based semi-local method is more consistent, accurate, skillful, reliable, and has higher resolution than the global approach. However, only the global method provides better discrimination in predicting the probability of high-precipitation events compared to the semi-local method. Additionally, the EMOS-CSG model outperforms BMA model for upper thresholds, and BMA model outperforms EMOS-CSG for lower thresholds. 
"

The calibration of precipitation forecasts from the WRF system in Iran is carried out using two approaches: semi-local and global parameter estimation for the BMA and EMOS-CSG models. The forecast skill of the global and semi-local methods is evaluated based on clustering observation stations for the BMA and EMOS-CSG models. Various criteria including consistency, accuracy, skill, reliability, resolution, and discrimination are measured for the four models: global BMA, semi-local BMA, global EMOS-CSG, and semi-local EMOS-CSG. The PIT, BS, BSS, Reliability diagram, ROC diagram, and RSS are utilized to assess these criteria.

The results indicate that, overall, the clustering-based semi-local method demonstrates higher levels of consistency, accuracy, skill, reliability, and resolution compared to the global approach. However, only the global method exhibits better discrimination in predicting the probability of high-precipitation events compared to the semi-local method. Additionally, the EMOS-CSG model outperforms the BMA model for upper thresholds, while the BMA model outperforms the EMOS-CSG model for lower thresholds.

edit the text as first sentence of abstract: "The Ensemble Prediction System are typically given in the form of forecast ensembles obtained from multiple runs of numerical weather prediction models with varying initial conditions and physics parameterizations. Such ensemble predictions tend to be biased and underdispersive and thus require statistical postprocessing."

Ensemble Prediction Systems often provide forecast ensembles generated by multiple runs of numerical weather prediction models, incorporating different initial conditions and physics parameterizations. However, these ensemble predictions are commonly biased and underdispersive, necessitating the application of statistical postprocessing techniques.

edit the text as peace of   abstract: "Different versions of the ensemble model output statistics (EMOS) and the Bayesian model averaging (BMA) post-processing methods are used in the present paper to calibrate 24, 48 and 72 hr forecasts of 24 hr accumulative precipitation.  The ensemble employs the weather and research forecasting (WRF) model with eight different configurations which were run over Iran for six months (September 2015–February 2016).  This article proposes two semi-local methods for estimating the EMOS and BMA coefficients where the training data for a specific observation station are augmented with corresponding forecast cases from stations with similar characteristics. Similarities between stations are determined using either distance functions or clustering based on various features of the climatology, forecast errors, ensemble predictions and locations of the observation stations. "

In this study, different versions of the ensemble model output statistics (EMOS) and Bayesian model averaging (BMA) post-processing methods are employed to calibrate 24, 48, and 72-hour forecasts of 24-hour accumulated precipitation. The ensemble consists of the weather and research forecasting (WRF) model with eight different configurations, executed over Iran for a duration of six months (September 2015–February 2016).

This article introduces two semi-local methods for estimating the EMOS and BMA coefficients. These methods involve augmenting the training data for a specific observation station with corresponding forecast cases from stations exhibiting similar characteristics. Station similarities are determined through the use of distance functions or clustering techniques that consider various features of climatology, forecast errors, ensemble predictions, and observation station locations.

The ensemble employs the weather and research forecasting (WRF) model with eight different configurations which were run over Iran for six months (September 2015–February 2016). This article proposes two  methods global and semi-local for estimating the EMOS and BMA coefficients where the training data for a specific observation station are augmented with corresponding forecast cases from stations with similar characteristics. Similarities between stations are determined using either distance functions or clustering based on various features of the climatology, forecast errors, ensemble predictions and locations of the observation stations. "
